unitTitle: Y7 Programming essentials in Scratch — part II (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: You've got the moves! — subroutines & decomposition
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a subroutine is (a group of instructions that runs when it is called)
I can say what decomposition means (breaking a problem into smaller parts)
I can use subroutines to break a dance into separate moves
I can make four subroutines for a dance in Scratch
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: subroutine · decomposition · subproblem · call. TA: everyone has Scratch open + the Dance Battle project (ncce.io/DanceBattle). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — custom blocks ARE subroutines. Show the `define twist` / `define jump_spin` code; pupils predict and TICK what a subroutine is on the starter worksheet (no writing for Support).
   • Support: tick what a subroutine is.  • Core: say what `twist` does when called.  • Challenge: say why each move gets its own subroutine.
2. (5–10) We-do — unplugged subroutines. Four volunteers each hold a move card (name to the class, instructions to themselves); call them in order, then at random. Point: once it works, you only need the NAME.
3. (10–18) Decomposition — break the “dance battle” problem into one subroutine per move. Likely error: “a subroutine runs on its own” → fix-words “a subroutine only runs when it is CALLED.”
4. (18–33) You-do — Plan four moves on the activity worksheet using the nine costumes.
   • Support: pick costume numbers from the strip.  • Core: design unaided.  • Challenge: add jumps/spins. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (33–50) You-do — make a custom block for each move and call it with a key. Keep a screenshot of the four subroutines.
6. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; pairs show their four dance subroutines. TA notes who met all four.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Fly cat, fly! — condition-controlled iteration
lessons.objectives:
I can say where a condition-controlled loop (repeat until) is useful
I can predict what a repeat until loop will do
I can use a repeat until loop in a program
I can change a program so a sprite stops at the right place
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · condition · condition-controlled · repeat until. TA: everyone has Scratch + the Fly cat, fly game (ncce.io/flycatfly). This lesson is PRIMM (Predict · Run · Investigate · Modify). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — PREDICT the building sprite code (forever → set x 280 → next costume → repeat until x position < -220 → change x by -10). Pupils write a prediction; Support ticks what `repeat until` does.
2. (5–10) RUN — open the project with a partner; were the predictions right? Keep it verbal and low-stakes.
3. (10–30) INVESTIGATE — three-part worksheet (with teacher / with partner / alone): how x works, what Next costume does, when the Forever and Repeat until loops stop.
   • Support: tick (y = up/down; fall = inside a forever loop).  • Core: explain Next costume / why the loop restarts.  • Challenge: why repeat until beats forever for stopping; write the stopping condition.
4. (30–48) MODIFY (pair programming, swap roles every 5 min) — add up/down movement, add gravity, then swap forever → repeat until <y position < -180> so the cat stops. Order-the-code Parsons supports this on the worksheet. Likely error / misconception M9: thinks the condition is checked once → fix-words “the condition is checked every time round; it exits the instant it is true.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; targeted questions: explain forever vs repeat until. Keep a screenshot of the modified code.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Loop the loop! — choosing the right iteration
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what each type of loop does (forever, repeat, repeat until)
I can say whether a loop is count-controlled or condition-controlled
I can evaluate which loop is best for a task
I can justify my choice of loop
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · count-controlled · condition-controlled. TA: everyone has Scratch + Tera’s adventure (ncce.io/TerasAdventure). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — describe the three loops. Show the count-controlled repeat (4) square. Pupils MATCH each loop to what it does (matching question, no writing for Support).
   • Support: match loop → behaviour.  • Core: is repeat (4) count- or condition-controlled?  • Challenge: explain the difference between the two kinds.
2. (5–25) We-do — investigate the loops in Tera’s adventure (pair programming, swap every 5 min). Targeted questions on individual loops; they don’t need the whole game.
3. (25–40) You-do — EVALUATE three scenarios (draw one square / ten-second countdown / dance until stopped). Pupils choose the best loop and justify it. Scenario 1 answer: repeat (4) — draws it once. Do the first together if needed.
   • Support: choose the loop that draws one square.  • Core: choose + explain for the countdown.  • Challenge: invent a task for each loop type. Likely error: picks forever for everything → fix-words “forever never stops — only when the code runs the whole game.”
4. (40–52) Keep a screenshot of a loop investigated/changed (show-your-work).
5. (52–55) Plenary — one fact about loops each; listen for gaps; review summary.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Treasure those lists! — using lists
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a list is (a collection of related items with one name)
I can describe why a program needs lists, not just variables
I can identify when a list is being used in a program
I can use a list (add, delete, replace, check an item)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: list · variable. TA: everyone has Scratch + Legends of Scratch (ncce.io/legendofscratch). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–3) Starter — which loop draws ONE pentagon? Show the repeat (5) pentagon. Recall from last lesson; Support ticks the best loop; Core/Challenge bridge to “why a variable can’t hold a whole list.”
2. (3–11) I-do — demonstrate the shopping-list project (ncce.io/shoppinglist). Trace the `item` variable on the board (eggs → cheese → flour): each value overwrites the variable, but all stay in the list.
3. (11–46) You-do — play Legends of Scratch for 5 minutes (be strict), then INVESTIGATE the inventory list with the activity worksheet: items are added (apple), deleted (watermelon→monkey), replaced (apple→diamond), checked (goblin/centaur). An order-the-code Parsons rehearses an add/clear/replace sequence.
   • Support: tick what a list is / what happens to the apple.  • Core: why a list beats a variable; what the monkey does to the watermelon.  • Challenge: what the goblin checks; a list idea of their own. Likely error: confuses replace with add → fix-words “add puts a NEW item on the end; replace swaps the one already there.”
4. (46–50) Show-your-work — modify the cake sprite to add the cake to the inventory; screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — match the description to the right list block; self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Translate this! (Part 1) — decompose the translation quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what decomposition means
I can break a large problem into smaller subproblems (tasks)
I can translate a word into another language using the Translate extension
I can start building my translation quiz in Scratch
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: decomposition · subproblem. TA: everyone has Scratch; support sheets (Tasks 1–6) and the rubric ready. Paired project across Lessons 11 and 12. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — give an example of decomposition (think · write · pair · share). Support ticks what decomposition means. Remind: a problem feels too hard when it hasn’t been decomposed enough.
2. (5–10) We-do — introduce the scenario (a ten-word translation quiz for the MFL teacher). The problem is already split into tasks; pairs predict where they’ll get stuck (usually Task 4).
3. (10–20) We-do — decompose Task 4a together: install the Translate extension → say a translation → store a word in a variable → translate the variable. An order-the-code Parsons rehearses this on the worksheet.
   • Support: tick which extension translates a word.  • Core: what the translate block does; why break Task 4 down.  • Challenge: name two remaining subproblems; how to increment the score only when correct.
4. (20–50) You-do — start the project in pairs; hand out support sheets as scaffolding; go through the rubric. Likely error: translation stays in English → fix-words “check the language dropdown is set.” TA: prompt with the support sheet, don’t fix it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — one thing easy, one thing tricky, swap advice; self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task (scaffolding via support sheets); no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Translate this! (Part 2) — build & assess the translation quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can use a loop and a list to test ten words
I can keep and show a score in my quiz
I can use a subroutine to organise my program
I can check my work against a rubric
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: decomposition · loop · list · subroutine · score. TA: same pairs as Lesson 11; support sheets, rubric, and the summative quiz ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what do the Translation blocks do, and how will you use them? (think · write · pair · share). Support ticks where the word is stored.
2. (5–35) You-do — continue the project with your partner. Aim for the main solution (ncce.io/translationsolution); extensions optional. A short quick-review (decomposition / count- vs condition-controlled / repeat until) sits on the worksheet. Likely error: the score goes up every turn → fix-words “only change the score inside the ‘if answer is correct’ part.” TA: prompt with the support sheet.
   • Support: tick that a loop avoids copying blocks ten times.  • Core: when the score should go up; where the word list is set up.  • Challenge: play-again and wrong-words extensions.
3. (35–40) Peer — swap with another pair, share one win and one tricky part; self/peer-assess against the rubric checklist.
4. (40–55) Summative — short multiple-choice quiz across the Y7 programming units (present as suits the class, e.g. a Google Form). Run in a separate lesson if pairs need more build time.
5. Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; show the finished quiz. Celebrate effort on a hard, large project.
Differentiation on the SAME task (scaffolding via support sheets); no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.
